title: Is Fed Chair Warsh’s sanguine take on US inflation dynamics appropriate?

Happy Wednesday out there team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our macro minute for Wednesday,
July 1st, 2026. As always, we start with
the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
is Fed chair Kevin Warsh's sanguine take
on the US inflation dynamics
appropriate? The short answer is no, at
least regarding core inflation. Peaking
inflation, which is the current trade,
is not the same thing as sticky
inflation, the likely next trade.
In terms of the analytical nuance, the
May jobs report did not disrupt the low
higher low fire narrative characterizing
the US labor market. Cyclically and
structurally depressed labor market
turnover has weighed heavily upon wage
growth in recent years. The June ADP
employment report signaled an ebbing of
this dynamic. While it's too early to
tell if this is the start of a sustained
uptrend in wage growth, this is the
appropriate question to be asking at the
current juncture, especially with the
civilian labor force labor supply
contracting at a well below trend rate
of minus 1.7% on a six-month annualized
basis, and private sector job openings
labor demand expanding at a well above
trend rate of 19.6%
on a six-month annualized basis.
Reminder, with labor force growth
projected to slow from a long-run mean
of 1.4% to 0.2% in 2026, the bulk of
economic growth must come from
productivity. Reminder, the latest
reading of non-farm productivity growth
featured a strong negative impulse to a
well below trend quarter-over-quarter
SAAR rate of 0.3%.
While we believe this multi-quarter
slowdown in productivity growth is
transitory, it did happen and it is
contributing to a tighter labor market
than is currently appreciated by
consensus. Reminder, the massive
aggregate demand shock from hyperscale
AI CapEx represents 2.4% and 3.5% of US
nominal GDP in 2026 and 2027,
respectively. The nominal year-over-year
growth rates of plus 350 billion and
plus 400 billion currently represent a
fifth of expected US nominal GDP growth
this year and over a quarter of expected
US nominal GDP growth next year.
Reminder, the massive aggregate demand
shock from AI CapEx coincides with a
modestly tight labor market, according
to the minus 20 basis points spread
between the U3 headline unemployment
rate and May brew, and a mildly
overheating economy according to the
current positive 110 basis point output
gap. All told, the US labor market
continues to confound both investors and
policy makers alike because of two
competing dynamics. First, from a
cyclical perspective, the massive
aggregate demand shock from AI CapEx and
procyclical fiscal easing from Abba are
contributing to a recovery in employment
growth.
The June ADP employment report featured
a strong positive impulse in core
private sector employment to a below
trend 3-month SAR of 0.6%, which was the
highest reading since January of last
year. Second, from a structural
perspective, the uptrending JOLTS total
job openings to total unemployed workers
ratio, long-term unemployed ratio, and
private sector job openings rate private
sector hires rate spread signal that
underlying weakness is being masked by
AI CapEx and procyclical fiscal
stimulus. Time will tell if the recovery
in private sector job openings uh it
represents legitimate demand for labor
or a mere or a mere phantom job listings
designed to influence the narrative
surrounding AI. Recall that private
sector job openings uh increased to
6.794 million, which was the highest
since March of 2024.
And finally, Microsoft will soon
announce another Finally and fiddling,
rather. Microsoft will soon announce
another round of layoffs to curb costs.
Thousands of positions across sales,
consulting, and Xbox will be eliminated.
Reductions will likely be capped at 2.5%
of a 220,000 person staff. Last year,
6,000 roles were cut in May, plus
another 9,000 downsizing in July.
Microsoft also rolled out voluntary
buyouts this year. Roughly 7% of its
125,000 US staff, nearly 9,000,
qualified, and 1/3 accepted, matching
forecasts. So, very, very complicated
labor market dynamics right now, but in
our view, we think
the the labor market in the in its
current, you know, condition, is
actually contributing to core inflation
pressure right now. And this is on top
of, you know, the policy dynamics, the
policy drivers of inflation contributing
to core inflationary pressure, the
monetary drivers of inflation
contributing to core inflationary
pressure.
And ultimately, uh you got this
pro-cyclical uh fiscal stimulus and a
massive aggregate demand shock from AI
CapEx. So, that's four independent
vectors that are all contributing to
upward pressure on core inflation. And
and and if you can add a fifth vector,
which is the Fed's now accommodative
monetary policy setting. So, uh in our
opinion, we think Kevin Warsh is a
sanguine take on US inflation dynamics
is not appropriate, cuz particularly
regarding core inflation. Obviously, uh
crude oil represents a meaningful
disinflation, uh pending disinflation to
headline inflation, but again, uh we're
going to get to a point in the next few
months, in our opinion, uh where uh the
Fed and and investors are going to be
disappointed uh with the rate of
disinflation. In our opinion, we think
that's the next likely trade, the likely
next trade in financial markets, uh if
uh another trade doesn't materialize
before then, which may or may not be uh
peak rate of change of AI CapEx. Refer
to my commentary on Schwab this morning
for more details on that. So, as always,
I wrap up with a question from our
community. This was titled Bitcoin bear
markets. It says, "Hi team. I hope you
are well.
Uh thanks again for being such amazing
stewards of our wealth. I cannot
emphasize enough how much time and
stress you are saving me. So, thanks for
the kind feedback." Says, "I know we are
not in the business of timing bottoms,
and I do not plan to for- front run the
signal, but I wonder, uh say, if Bitcoin
goes down 60 to 70% from the highs, and
then KISS at some point instructs us to
buy again, um uh with a max allocation
10%, does that present a {quote}
superior buying opportunity that
warrants considering increasing the
allocation from say 10% to 20%? I
imagine this positioning would be very
bearish after such a deep correction. I
know we are allowed to customize KISS.
My question is more theoretical. Uh
basically what I'm asking is if uh
outsize bear positioning justifies
considering increasing exposure to the
asset. Would love to hear your thoughts.
All the best. So, excellent question.
You know, the obviously the first
question is sort of the first answer uh
to the the the the the main answer to
the question is sort of implied already
in the in the in the prose, which is you
know, we're not going to change KISS.
The structural nature of KISS uh is very
thoughtfully constructed. Obviously we
spent, you know, years doing research to
produce uh the the the you know, the the
the output that is KISS currently um the
current version of KISS. So, we're not
going to change the target allocation to
Bitcoin uh just because Bitcoin has gone
down. Uh Bitcoin the target allocation
is is is maxed out at
is is fixed at 10% currently. Uh and
then it obviously, you know, the the
dynamic position sizing and and and ball
targeting elements will, you know, guide
up and guide down its exposure. Uh and
it continues to, you know, it's been
I don't think any system has traded
Bitcoin as well as we have since we
started this firm uh back in in in the
spring of 2021. Recall that uh Bitcoin
If you just look at our KISS's last two
trades on Bitcoin, uh booked a 22% gain
back in in October, I think October of
last year. Bitcoin's down about 40% uh
since there. And then Bitcoin uh we had
a little cup of coffee being long
Bitcoin this spring this year when it
was rallying. Uh but then uh it reversed
obviously that rally uh until we booked
a modest loss of about 3%. Uh and
Bitcoin's down about 20% since then. So,
uh Bitcoin, you know, we know how to
book the gains of Bitcoin. We know how
to create a positive skew return
distribution not just in the Bitcoin
asset class, but in every asset class in
terms of risk management overlays. And
so, we expect that to be uh the case
going forward. Uh so, when we get to the
bottom of this Bitcoin bear market and
KISS the doctor will finally tell us to,
you know, allocate to get to max
position sizes uh in Bitcoin near that
bottom, you know, shortly after that
bottom, then uh you know, that would
warrant if an investor wanted to have
more exposure uh to the digital asset
class or just more exposure to risk
assets broadly, uh that that would
warrant, you know, someone implementing
a customized version of KISS that say
takes their target allocation beyond
what KISS is target allocation to
Bitcoin is at 10%. So, you know, someone
can easily implement a customized
version of KIS if you want some insights
on how to do that, just go to the KIS
FAQ here and then find the um
find the uh there's a if I want to
implement a customized there's a
question that says if I want to
implement a customized version of KIS in
my portfolio, how do I calculate uh the
exposure of my preferred ETFs? And then
we walk you through all that and all the
information you need to do that you
know, in terms of where to find the
information you need to do that to
maintain that. So, uh we have thousands
of questions in the world all around the
world following KIS, following
customized versions of KIS that are more
suitable for their investment
preferences, risk tolerances, and
strategic investment objectives and
we're super proud of that. So, we'll
wrap it up there. Darius here presenting
our macro minute for Wednesday, July
1st, 2026. Best of luck out there today.
